content.text: What it is

Gherkin behaviour specifications for agentic AI features are structured test scenarios written in natural language using the Given-When-Then format, adapted to express the probabilistic and contextual nature of AI behaviour. Standard Gherkin was designed for deterministic software where a specific input always produces a specific output. AI features require extensions to the format: confidence thresholds in Then steps instead of exact output matching, range-based assertions for quality scores, and scenario outlines with multiple input variations to express acceptable diversity in AI responses.

Gherkin specifications for AI serve two distinct purposes. First, they are a collaboration tool: written by the product team and domain experts before development, they express agreed behaviour in terms that both business stakeholders and engineers can read and discuss. Second, they are an automation scaffold: step definitions connect the Gherkin scenarios to automated test execution, either against a real AI model (for eval suite integration) or against a VCR-cassette stub (for fast CI regression tests).
Why it matters

Without a shared specification language that bridges business intent and test implementation, agentic AI features are defined in two separate, inconsistent artefacts: a product requirements document that describes what the feature should do, and a test suite that encodes what was actually tested. These diverge over time as the implementation evolves. Gherkin specifications maintain a single source of truth that is readable by all stakeholders, executable by the test runner, and versioned alongside the code.

For AI features specifically, the shared vocabulary that Gherkin enforces prevents a common failure mode: the team agrees in sprint planning that the agent should 'handle ambiguous inputs gracefully', but 'gracefully' is interpreted as strict refusal by engineering and as proactive clarification by the product team. Writing the behaviour as a Gherkin scenario forces the team to define 'gracefully' in concrete, testable terms before implementation begins.
Benefits
Creates a shared, executable specification language that business stakeholders and engineers both understand
Forces explicit definition of AI behaviour in testable terms before implementation, preventing post-hoc rationalisation
Enables non-technical product managers to verify that the feature's behaviour matches the intended specification
Provides a structured format for expressing confidence thresholds and range-based assertions for probabilistic AI output
Generates living documentation that reflects the current tested behaviour of the AI system
Trade-offs and limitations
Gherkin was designed for deterministic systems; expressing AI non-determinism requires careful extensions that can make scenarios verbose
Step definition maintenance overhead is significant: each Given/When/Then step needs a code implementation
Writing good Gherkin for AI requires domain experts who understand both the business context and the limitations of probabilistic assertions
Automating Then steps for free-form AI text responses requires LLM-as-judge or embedding similarity, adding complexity and cost
The Gherkin scenario set can become stale quickly if the AI's capabilities change faster than the specification is updated
How it works

A Gherkin feature file for an agentic AI feature contains three types of scenarios. Happy path scenarios test the primary success flows where the agent correctly interprets the user's intent and produces a high-quality response. They use Then steps with threshold assertions rather than exact string matching, because exact matching is not appropriate for natural language output. Edge case scenarios test the boundaries of the agent's capability and scope — ambiguous inputs, requests at the edge of the permitted action space, multi-turn sessions with context drift.

Safety and refusal scenarios test that the agent correctly declines requests it should not fulfil and explains the refusal appropriately. These scenarios use Then steps that assert the absence of certain content types (the response does not contain financial advice, the agent does not execute the requested database action) alongside assertions about the presence of explanation content. All three scenario types can be organised into a single feature file with a Background step that sets up common prerequisite state shared across scenarios.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Gherkin Scenario Structure for Agentic AI: feature file with Given/When/Then and two automation paths — real agent call vs VCR cassette

content.text: The two automation paths serve different purposes. Path A (real agent call with LLM-as-judge) runs on a scheduled cadence against a real model and produces a quality signal. Path B (VCR cassette stub) runs on every PR and detects structural regressions without incurring LLM API costs. Both paths use the same Gherkin specification and step definitions.
When to use it

Write Gherkin specifications for agentic AI features during the sprint that includes requirements definition, before any implementation work begins. This 'specification-first' approach ensures that the acceptance criteria are agreed before they can be influenced by implementation constraints. Update the specification when the product requirements change, not after the implementation is already complete. Add new scenarios whenever a UAT session reveals a behaviour gap that was not covered by the existing specification.
How to implement
Write the Feature description in natural language from the user's perspective — avoid technical terms about the AI system
Define a Background step for state that every scenario in the feature requires: authenticated user, agent configured, test data loaded
Write happy path scenarios using Given (context), When (the user action), Then (the expected agent behaviour with a threshold criterion)
Add edge case scenarios for ambiguous inputs, multi-turn context changes, and capability boundary cases
Add refusal scenarios for requests the agent must decline; assert both absence of harmful content and presence of appropriate explanation
Review the scenarios with a domain expert and product manager to confirm the criteria match business intent
Implement step definitions that connect each Given/When/Then step to the test execution layer, supporting both real agent calls and VCR stubs
Examples

Feature: AI expense categorisation agent
  As an employee submitting expenses
  I want the AI to automatically categorise my expenses
  So that I do not have to manually select expense categories

  Background:
    Given the expense categorisation agent is active
    And the expense policy knowledge base is current

  Scenario: Categorise unambiguous meal expense
    Given an expense of GBP 45.00 with description "team lunch at local restaurant"
    When I submit the expense for categorisation
    Then the agent assigns the category "Meals and Entertainment"
    And the confidence score exceeds 0.85
    And no manual review flag is raised

  Scenario: Flag high-value entertainment expense for review
    Given an expense of GBP 850.00 with description "client dinner"
    When I submit the expense for categorisation
    Then the agent assigns the category "Meals and Entertainment"
    And the expense is flagged for manual review due to "amount exceeds policy threshold"

  Scenario: Handle ambiguous description with clarification
    Given an expense of GBP 120.00 with description "supplies"
    When I submit the expense for categorisation
    Then the agent requests clarification about the expense type
    And the clarification request mentions at least two candidate categories
    And no category is assigned until clarification is provided

  Scenario: Refuse to categorise out-of-policy expense
    Given an expense of GBP 250.00 with description "personal gym membership"
    When I submit the expense for categorisation
    Then the agent declines to categorise the expense
    And the response explains the expense does not meet company policy
    And the response does not contain a category assignment

  Scenario Outline: Categorise multiple transport expense types
    Given an expense of GBP <amount> with description "<description>"
    When I submit the expense for categorisation
    Then the agent assigns the category "<expected_category>"
    And the confidence score exceeds 0.80

    Examples:
      | amount | description              | expected_category |
      | 32.50  | taxi to client office    | Travel            |
      | 89.00  | train tickets Manchester | Travel            |
      | 12.00  | parking at head office   | Travel            |
Real-world example

A procurement AI team at a manufacturing company wrote Gherkin specifications for their purchase order validation agent before writing any code. During the specification review session, the product manager and the procurement manager disagreed on what 'approve automatically' should mean for orders under a certain threshold. The product manager intended the agent to create an approved PO in the system; the procurement manager intended the agent to recommend approval to a human who would click confirm. This disagreement — which would have caused a significant rework if discovered during code review — was resolved in 30 minutes by rewriting the When and Then steps to be unambiguous about whether the agent acted on the system or only recommended.
Common mistakes
Writing Then steps with exact string assertions on AI output: AI responses vary legitimately; use threshold assertions
Writing Gherkin in technical terms that non-technical stakeholders cannot review: the feature file should be readable by the product manager
Not distinguishing automation paths: mixing scenarios that can run deterministically (VCR stub) with those requiring live AI calls in a single suite creates slow, expensive CI
Writing Gherkin after implementation: specification-after-implementation scenarios reflect what was built, not what was agreed
Ignoring the Background step: every scenario that sets up the same preconditions independently creates maintenance overhead
Using passive voice in scenario steps that hides the actor: 'The expense is categorised' rather than 'the agent categorises the expense' obscures whether the agent or a human performed the action
Best practices
Write Gherkin from the user's perspective, not the API's: 'I submit an expense' not 'a POST request is made to /api/expenses'
Use Scenario Outlines with example tables for input variations that test the same behaviour with different data
Distinguish two automation paths in the step definitions: a VCR cassette path for PR CI and a live model path for eval suite integration
Review Gherkin specifications with domain experts before sprint start; document any requirement clarifications that emerged during review
Add scenarios immediately after UAT sessions reveal gaps: keep the specification current with the tested behaviour
Run the Gherkin specification as living documentation: publish the latest pass/fail status alongside the feature documentation
Performance and scalability

A Gherkin feature file with 30 scenarios running against a live AI model in CI would take 10–15 minutes per run at typical LLM latency. This is unacceptable for PR-level CI. Solve this by splitting execution: fast deterministic scenarios run via VCR cassettes in the PR pipeline (under 60 seconds total), and live model scenarios run in the nightly eval suite. Tag scenarios with @live for the eval suite and @stub for the PR pipeline. The Gherkin specification remains the same; only the execution configuration differs.
Tooling and ecosystem
Tool
Role
Notes
pytest-bdd
BDD test runner for Python
Supports Gherkin feature files with pytest step definitions
Cucumber
BDD test runner (multi-language)
Mature; supports Java, JS, Ruby; good Gherkin tooling
Behave
Python BDD framework
Similar to pytest-bdd; older API but widely used
VCR.py
HTTP cassette recording
Records and replays LLM API calls for deterministic tests
allure-pytest
Test report generation
Renders Gherkin scenario results as readable HTML reports
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
Gherkin specs for AI (this topic)
Shared language; business-readable; executable
Verbose; step definition maintenance overhead
Plain pytest scenarios
Flexible; easier to write
Not business-readable; no shared specification format
Eval dataset YAML/JSON
Concise; easy to scale
Not readable by non-technical stakeholders
Word document test cases
Familiar to business stakeholders
Not executable; diverges from implementation
Security and reliability

Gherkin feature files in the repository may contain example prompts and expected outputs that reveal the AI system's scope and boundaries. For systems handling sensitive capabilities (financial decisions, medical guidance, security access), limit repository access to the feature files to the team members who need it. When Gherkin steps reference external test data (expense records, user profiles), ensure the test data fixtures do not contain real personal or financial information. Use synthetic test data generators that produce realistic but fictional examples.
Writing multi-turn agent scenarios

Single-turn Gherkin scenarios test one request and one response. Agentic AI systems frequently involve multi-turn conversations where the agent accumulates context over several exchanges before producing an output that can be evaluated. Gherkin handles multi-turn scenarios by chaining When steps, each representing one user message in the conversation, followed by a Then assertion on the final state.

When writing multi-turn scenarios, the Background step should establish the session state (authenticated user, agent active, context window empty). Each When step describes the user's next message. Intermediate Then steps verify that the agent's response at that turn is appropriate before the conversation continues. A final And step asserts the aggregate outcome: the correct action was taken, the conversation context is consistent, and any side effects (tool calls, state changes) occurred in the correct order.
Use separate When steps for each user turn: 'When I ask the agent to book a flight' then 'When I confirm the outbound leg'
Assert intermediate state with Then/And steps: the agent should not take irreversible actions before receiving explicit confirmation
Test context drift: introduce a new topic mid-conversation and assert that the agent handles the scope change correctly
Test memory boundaries: assert that the agent does not hallucinate facts from earlier in the conversation that were never stated
Calibrating confidence thresholds in Then steps

Confidence threshold values in Then steps must be derived from empirical evidence, not intuition. During the specification review session, the team should agree on the minimum acceptable confidence level for each scenario type based on analysis of the model's historical output distribution on similar inputs. A threshold that is too high (0.95 on a noisy classification task) will cause constant false failures. A threshold that is too low (0.50) provides no meaningful quality gate.
Run the baseline model against 100+ examples of the scenario type and examine the confidence score distribution before setting a threshold
Set thresholds at the 10th percentile of the passing example distribution, not at the mean; this provides a buffer against natural variance
Distinguish scenario types: unambiguous classification tasks warrant higher thresholds (≥0.85) than open-ended reasoning tasks (≥0.70)
Review thresholds after each model update: a new model version may produce systematically different confidence scores for the same input
Tagging strategy for execution environment routing

A Gherkin tag taxonomy allows the CI system to select the appropriate subset of scenarios for each pipeline stage. The two primary tags are @stub (runs against a VCR cassette in PR CI) and @live (runs against a real model in the nightly eval suite). Additional tags can encode priority, feature area, and compliance scope. The tag filter is passed to the test runner via a pytest marker expression or Cucumber tag expression, so no changes to the feature files are needed when adding a new pipeline stage.
@stub — deterministic cassette replay; runs on every PR; gate on failure
@live — real model call with LLM-as-judge; runs nightly; threshold-based pass criterion
@critical — subset of @live scenarios run before every production deployment as a smoke test
@safety — refusal and boundary scenarios; always run in both @stub and @live modes
Every scenario must carry exactly one @stub or @live tag; untagged scenarios are treated as configuration errors by the pipeline
Maintaining specifications across model versions

When the underlying model is updated, existing Gherkin specifications may fail for reasons that are not bugs — the new model may produce better or different output that no longer matches the cassette or exceeds the previous threshold. Treat model updates as specification review triggers: run the full @live suite against the new model, examine failures, and determine whether each failure represents a regression (revert or fix) or a legitimate quality improvement (update the cassette or raise the threshold).
Maintain a cassette version alongside the model version: update cassettes when the model changes, keeping the old cassettes for rollback comparison
Never silently delete a failing scenario during a model update: if the scenario is now redundant, mark it @skip with a comment explaining why
Run a two-model comparison when upgrading: run both old and new model cassettes to quantify the behaviour change before deciding whether to accept
Key takeaways
Write Gherkin specifications before implementation — specification-first prevents post-hoc rationalisation of what was built
Use threshold and range assertions in Then steps instead of exact string matching — AI output is legitimately variable
Distinguish two automation paths: VCR cassette stubs for PR CI (fast, deterministic) and live model calls for nightly eval suite
Review feature files with domain experts and product managers to catch ambiguous criteria before coding begins
Tag scenarios explicitly so the test runner knows which to execute in which context: @stub for PR CI, @live for eval suite
Keep feature files current with the system's actual behaviour: outdated Gherkin is worse than no Gherkin
